title: Chapter 25: Underlayer Economics
content: The Underlayer was hungry. Always hungry.
Wren learned this on her fifth morning, when Boz handed her a dented canteen and said: "Water run. Southeast junction, two hours. Bring back what you can carry."
The canteen tasted like rust. The water inside was lukewarm and metallic. She drank it anyway.

Southeast junction turned out to be a maintenance hub where three tunnels converged around a defunct filtration system. The system still worked—barely—tricking groundwater through ancient membranes into something almost potable. Two invisibles Wren didn't recognize were already there, filling containers.
Everything in the Underlayer operated on a simple equation: take what you need, leave what you can, don't ask where it came from.

The invisibles survived on scavenging. Abandoned infrastructure. City systems too old to monitor, too expensive to dismantle. Gaps in the digital architecture that people like them—people who flickered at the edges of recognition—could slip through.

Food came from loading docks after hours. Medicine from pharmacy stockrooms where inventory was counted quarterly and discrepancies blamed on software. Clothing from donation bins that nobody audited.
The city above produced enough waste to sustain a shadow population forever. It just didn't know it.
"Watch this," Boz said.
He stood at the edge of a platform overlooking a service corridor. Below, a conveyor belt carried sealed crates toward a distribution hub—food shipments headed for the surface districts.

Boz reached down with one hand. His Stage 1 gave him just enough visibility to interact with physical objects, but not enough for the conveyor's motion sensors to register a disruption. The light barrier didn't trip. The alarms didn't sound.
He grabbed a crate as it passed and pulled it onto the platform. Pried it open. Inside: vacuum-sealed meal packs, fifty of them, stamped with the logo of a city-contrusted meal service.
"Breakfast," Boz said.
Wren stared. "That's it? You just... take it?"

"The system sees a crate. The crate goes missing. The system registers a logistics error. The logistics error gets flagged for review. The review gets assigned to a mid-level employee who's already three weeks behind on quarterly reports. The employee marks it 'shrinkage, probable cause: equipment malfunction.' The crate never existed."
He handed her a meal pack. It was warm from the conveyor. The plastic crackled under her fingers.
"Eat," he said. "You're getting thin."
She was getting thin. She was also getting valuable.

It started with the door at the northwest passage. A security lock, newer than the Underlayer's infrastructure, tied into the city's central access grid. The invisibles had been circumventing it for weeks—literally going around through a flooded tunnel that added forty minutes to any trip.
Wren pressed her palm against the panel.
The lock clicked open.

Not because she hacked it. Because she glitched it—reached into the digital space between the lock's query and its response and introduced a moment of static. The system didn't register an unauthorized entry. It registered nothing. A gap in its own memory, too brief to flag.
She stood on the other side of the door, breathing hard. It had come easier this time. Less effort. Like flexing a muscle that was finally getting stronger.
Boz, watching from behind, let out a low whistle.
"Hell," he said. "That's useful."
Word traveled fast.

Within a day, three invisibles asked Wren for help. A locked storage room. A payment terminal that needed to misread a transaction. A pharmacy dispensing cabinet that required a digital signature she could counterfeit with a touch.

She did it all. Each glitch cost her something—a headache that crept up her temples, a ringing in her ears, a wobble in her own perception that made the world shimmer like a heat mirage. But she did it.
And every time she opened a door or bent a system, someone looked at her with something she'd never received in her old life.
Gratitude. Not performative—real. The kind that came from people who had nothing to give back except the acknowledgment that she'd made their lives fractionally less impossible.
Wren had spent years in an office full of people who saw her as competent. Efficient. Replaceable.
Down here, she was essential.
She'd never felt anything like it.

Ash found her on the third evening, sitting outside the northwest door she'd unlocked, legs dangling over the edge of the platform. The conveyor hummed below. She was eating a meal pack—her second that day—and staring at nothing.
"You've been busy," he said.
"People needed things."
"You've been busy," he repeated, and this time it meant something else.
Wren looked up at him. His tattoos glowed softly in the tunnel light, blue-green patterns shifting across his forearms. He dropped down beside her, close enough that their shoulders almost touched.
"Your glitch-push is getting stronger."
"Stage 3. It progresses."

"Mine did too. But not like this." He studied her face with an intensity that should have been uncomfortable. "You're not just opening locks. You're rewriting the architecture. I've seen you work. You're not pushing through the system—you're negotiating with it."
"That's a bit poetic for a data analyst."
"I was a good data analyst."
A silence. Then Ash said, carefully: "You're important to them now. To the community."
"I know."
"That's not always safe."
"What, being useful?"

"Being depended on." He picked at a loose thread on his sleeve. "When people need you, you stop making decisions for yourself. You start making them for the people who need you. And sometimes those things aren't the same."
Wren turned to face him. "Is this advice? Or a warning?"

"Observation." His tattoos pulsed. "I watched you glitch that pharmacy cabinet. You were gone for three seconds. Your body was here but you weren't—your consciousness was somewhere else, inside the system. If you push too hard, you might not come back."
"You worry about me."

"I worry about what happens to them if you burn out. They're building something around you. Dependence. Structure. Hope." He said the last word like it tasted bitter. "Hope is dangerous down here. It makes people forget how fragile everything is."
"Maybe fragility is the point," Wren said. "Maybe being needed is the only thing that makes any of this bearable."
Ash didn't answer. But he didn't move away either.
They sat on the platform, legs over the edge, watching the conveyor carry crates toward a world that didn't know they existed.
Wren felt the ache in her temples, the residual hum of the systems she'd touched. She felt the weight of the community settling onto her shoulders—not heavy, exactly.
Present.
For the first time in her life, someone needed her not for what she produced, but for what she was.
It terrified her.
She never wanted it to stop.
